cards.title: AI Code is Fast, But Messy: The Rise of AI 'Code Governors'
cards.thesis: While AI is proving incredibly powerful, even solving complex mathematical problems, developers are hitting major roadblocks with its reliability and output quality. From AI ignoring explicit instructions (like 'stop hooks' which tell an AI when to stop generating content) to producing bloated, unreadable code that clashes with existing 'messy' legacy systems, the promise of AI-driven coding is being undermined by a lack of control and predictability.

evidence.text: An amateur, armed with ChatGPT, managed to solve a complex Erdős problem, showing AI's incredible problem-solving capabilities.
A developer reported that Anthropic's Claude 4.7 is 'routinely ignoring the hook rules' that are meant to inject determinism into workflows, like preventing code commits if tests haven't run.

A senior developer asked if working with 'AI juniors' (junior developers relying heavily on AI) is 'becoming a nightmare,' noting that AI-generated code is often 100 lines for something that used to be a few, making it overblown and hard to argue against.

Developers are asking how to use AI code assistants on 'large messy legacy code bases,' recognizing that AI is 'extremely good at some things and actively terrible at others' when dealing with real-world, non-ideal code.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's hitting a wall where AI generates code that's either bloated, ignores specific instructions like 'stop hooks,' or just doesn't fit their messy legacy projects. The move is to build a 'code governor' service that acts as a quality control layer for AI-generated code. It would automatically check AI output against project-specific rules, style guides, and architectural patterns, then refactor or reject code that doesn't fit, helping developers get the speed of AI without the headaches.

cards.title: iPhone App Invasion: Why Headspace Is Silently Installing & What to Build Next
cards.thesis: Users are reporting that the Headspace app is silently installing itself on their iPhones every day, even when automatic downloads are turned off and their iOS is updated. This isn't just one or two people; multiple users across different iPhone models are experiencing this strange, unprompted app installation, sparking concerns about app control and privacy.

evidence.text: Every day for the past 3 days around 1pm EST the 'Headspace' app has been silently appearing on my iPhone (13 Pro).
Automatic downloads are turned off and I've updated to the latest iOS since this started happening.
I googled around and found a couple reddit threads with people reporting the exact same thing starting 2 or 3 days ago.
There were reports from people on iPhone 12 and iPhone 17 so it doesn't seem to be device specific.

cards.opportunity: People are panicking about Headspace silently installing on iPhones, but the real threat is *any* app doing it without your knowledge. There's a huge gap for a dead-simple utility that just monitors your installed apps list and sends you an immediate notification if a new one appears that you didn't approve. You could launch a basic version this weekend, tapping into the current paranoia around silent installs and charging a small one-time fee for peace of mind.

cards.title: Cash In On Prediction Markets: The Rise of Polymarket Copy Trading Bots
cards.thesis: People are really into automating their bets on Polymarket (a platform where you bet on future events like elections or crypto prices). There's a clear trend of builders creating 'copy trading' bots, which are programs that automatically mimic the trades of successful users. This means folks are looking for ways to make money in prediction markets without constantly watching the charts.

evidence.text: A GitHub repository for a Polymarket copy trading bot has seen significant interest, showing people want tools to automate their trading strategies.
Another independent project on GitHub also focuses on a Polymarket copy trading bot, indicating a growing community and demand for such automation.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's building the *bots* themselves, but nobody's making it dead simple for non-technical people to *use* them. Imagine an 'auto-follow' service where you just pick a Polymarket whale, set a budget, and it handles all the crypto wallet setup, bot deployment, and trade execution for you. You could launch a paid beta this weekend by manually running a few of these open-source bots for early users, proving the concept before building out the full automation.
